cross_connections.display_label: Her second cousin Martha married her husband's older brother Ezekiel.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-six leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail married James E. Cowles in August 1753 — same Farmington family network that produced her cousin Martha's marriage to James's older brother Ezekiel a decade earlier. She bore seven children between 1754 and 1771 and died at thirty-six in February 1772, eighteen months before her husband remarried Mary Bull.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her cousin married Cowles brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail and her second cousin Martha Hooker Cowles married brothers — Abigail to James E. Cowles in 1753, Martha to Ezekiel Cowles a decade earlier. Both Hooker cousins descended from Rev. Samuel Hooker through different sons; both Cowles brothers were sons of Capt. Isaac Cowles by his third wife Elizabeth Smith.

sources.notes: James E. Cowles is entry #118 in Cowles Genealogy.

marriages.spouse_name: Mary Bull Cowles (1736-1811) — built as X02856 in Stage 6Y-189 per new spouse-entry policy.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Cowles (1689-1767), Capt. Isaac Cowles's third wife, not built per Jalapeño Rule.

military_service.notes: Appointed Ensign May 1771; promoted Lieutenant May 1772 of the 1st Company / Trainband, 15th Connecticut Militia. Per Cowles Genealogy entry #118.

career.title: Inheritor of Capt. Isaac Cowles homestead
career.notes: Inherited from his father the place at the south end of Farmington Street (occupied around 1850 by James W. Cowles, Cowles Genealogy entry #743 — not built).

narrative_blocks.header: Brothers both married Hooker-side second cousins
narrative_blocks.body: James and his older brother Ezekiel both married women who were second cousins to each other through their Hooker line — Ezekiel to Martha Hooker in 1748, James to Abigail Hooker in 1753. The brothers' children grew up first cousins on the Cowles side and third cousins on the Hooker side.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant promotion came same month wife died
narrative_blocks.body: He was promoted from Ensign to Lieutenant of the 1st Company Trainband 15th Connecticut Militia in May 1772 — the same month his wife Abigail Hooker Cowles died. The personal-and-public events coincided in the heaviest weeks of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first wife 1772, remarried widow Mary Bull
narrative_blocks.body: Eighteen months after Abigail Hooker Cowles's February 1772 death, he married Mary Bull on 15 November 1773 — widow of William Lewis. The new wife brought stepchildren from her first marriage into the Farmington Cowles household.

sources.short: Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #614 Diadema Cowles Deming

narrative_blocks.header: Five of eight children died as infants
narrative_blocks.body: She and Chauncey Deming had eight children between 1784 and 1794; five died as infants or young children. The pattern included two infants named Samuel — the second born after the first had died — and three unnamed infants. Only Catharine (b.1787) and the first Samuel survived to adulthood; only Catharine produced descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Christmas Day birth in Farmington, 1756
narrative_blocks.body: She was born on Christmas Day 1756 at Farmington Hartford County CT — second child of James Cowles and Abigail Hooker Cowles. The birth occurred at the height of the French and Indian War, twenty years before Farmington would send its sons to the Revolutionary muster fields.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife of Farmington's wealthiest 1790-1825 merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Chauncey Deming was the dominant Farmington merchant of his generation, per Trumbull's 1886 Memorial History of Hartford County. She died at Farmington in September 1826 aged sixty-nine, fifteen months before Chauncey himself died in his seventieth year.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Deming of Plainville CT (not built per Jalapeño). Per EH#614 + WikiTree.
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Deming of Plainville CT (not built per Jalapeño). Per EH#614 + WikiTree.

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Active in the Farmington mercantile trade ~1780-1825 — buying goods at Hartford and New York, importing in his own vessels with cargo of West India and East India goods. Per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886): 'foremost in enterprise.'

career.title: Director of the Hartford Bank
career.notes: Active and influential director of the Hartford Bank per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886).

career.title: Director of a Middletown bank
career.organization: Middletown Bank (specific institution unspecified in source)
career.notes: Active and influential director of a Middletown bank per Trumbull's Memorial History (1886).

narrative_blocks.header: Held specie reserves to save Hartford Bank
narrative_blocks.body: During the War of 1812, all Connecticut banks except the Hartford Bank suspended specie payments. Chauncey held large reserves of hard currency in Farmington and produced them as needed to keep the Hartford Bank's credit intact. He served as a director of banks in both Hartford and Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: Powdered queue, blue coat, gilt buttons
narrative_blocks.body: Chauncey Deming was a strong rider and dressed for it. He galloped along Farmington Street on a 'strong and elegant horse,' and sat in church in a powdered queue, bright blue coat, and gilt buttons — a sartorial holdover from the eighteenth century preserved into the 1820s.

sources.short: Memorial History of Hartford County, Connecticut (Trumbull ed., 1886) — Unionville chapter by James L. Cowles
Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #614 Diadema Cowles Deming

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Diadema Cowles Deming.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #1580 Katherine Deming Cowles

narrative_blocks.header: Married her own fourth cousin Major Timothy Cowles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Major Timothy Cowles on 5 November 1809 — her own fourth cousin. Both descended from Rev Samuel Hooker through different sons, making this a documented cousin marriage uncommon enough to be flagged in family records.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father was the wealthiest man in Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Deming's father Chauncey Deming was not only Farmington's wealthiest resident but a soldier of the Revolution — one half of the famous firm of John and Chauncey Deming, merchants. Catherine was his only surviving daughter and brought considerable wealth to her marriage with Major Timothy Cowles in 1809.

narrative_blocks.header: 28502 dollar estate inventory at her 1859 death
narrative_blocks.body: Her will was proved 29 November 1859 with an inventory of $28,502.66 — substantial Farmington wealth from her father Chauncey Deming's mercantile legacy. She had joined the church only in 1827 at age forty, late for a colonial-Republican Farmington matron.

cross_connections.display_label: Married her fourth cousin — both descend Rev. Samuel Hooker.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Deming' carries her father's surname into the next generation. Her grandson Chauncey Deming Cowles (H01838) likewise carried 'Deming' as middle name.

narrative_blocks.header: A childless second marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Amy Cowles married the widower Joseph Andrews in 1806 at age 47 and the marriage produced no children. His eight children by his deceased first wife Lydia Judd were her stepchildren, most of them already grown or buried by the time she entered the household.

military_service.notes: Appointed Apr 23, 1777 under Lt. Col. Thomas Grosvenor. DAR Patriot. One of six brothers who served in the Revolutionary War.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children by his first wife
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph's eight children were all born to his first wife Lydia Judd between 1777 and 1794, four of whom died in childhood or infancy. His 1806 marriage to Amy Cowles — the Hooker descendant — came two years after Lydia's death and produced no children.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband was her first cousin via Capt. Isaac Cowles.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Elijah Cowles
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth and Elijah Jr. shared the same Cowles grandfather Capt. Isaac Cowles and grandmother Elizabeth Smith — Elizabeth's father James and Elijah's father Elijah Sr. were full brothers. Six children resulted; both Augustuses (the first an infant casualty, the second the merchant of J. & A. Cowles) and both Harriets (the first dead at eighteen, the second outliving everyone) carry her replacement-naming impulse.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Elijah Cowles Sr.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife was his first cousin via Capt. Isaac Cowles.

career.title: Merchant
career.notes: Senior partner with younger brother Augustus No. 2 (H01598) in J. & A. Cowles, Farmington CT.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his maternal grandfather James E. Cowles.
Named for his father Elijah Cowles Jr.

career.title: Merchant and farmer
career.notes: Junior partner with elder brother James

career.title: Cavalryman
career.organization: Farmington Cavalry troop under Col. William Rowe
career.notes: Member of Farmington's famous troop of

naming_inspiration.description: Named after deceased older brother Augustus (H01597, died as infant 1800). Replacement-naming pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: He bore a dead brother’s name
narrative_blocks.body: Named for an infant brother who had died three years before his birth, he ran the merchant house J. & A. Cowles with his brother James and rode in the Farmington cavalry, a noted figure in the town’s military life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages, no children, an early death
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife died at twenty-seven, four years into their marriage; sixteen years later he wed his cousin Eliza — and was dead himself within two years, leaving no children.

naming_inspiration.description: Named after deceased older sister Harriet (H01594, 1790-1808 died at 18). Born one year after sister's June 1808 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband drowned fording the Farmington river
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet married Charles Thompson of New York in November 1841. Thirteen years later — November 21, 1854 — Charles drowned while attempting to ford the Farmington river. Widowed at forty-five, Harriet outlived him by thirty years and died in December 1884.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery (Ancient Burying Ground), Farmington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington militia captain his entire life
narrative_blocks.body: He held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut militia and was known by that title for the rest of his life. He spent his entire life in Farmington — born there February 15, 1704/5 and dying there December 19, 1764, in his 60th year.

narrative_blocks.header: The big chair — militia saluted him sitting
narrative_blocks.body: In later years he became so corpulent he could not attend to business. On fine days a large chair was placed in the front yard near the door, where he sat smoking his pipe and visiting with passersby. When the local militia paraded, it was customary for them to march around by his house and salute him in the big chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Heir to the famous Farmington Hooker Elm
narrative_blocks.body: He was the son of Hon. John Hooker — the Farmington judge whose famous Hooker Elm gave the town its hospitality tradition. His grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker had ministered Farmington's church for thirty years; his great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford.

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Cheever (2nd wife of William Lewis III)
parents.father_research_notes: William Lewis III (b. Aug 19, 1620, d. Aug 18, 1690, Farmington). Son of William Lewis the Hartford Founder (b. 1594, d. 1683). First town clerk of Farmington after 1645 incorporation. Captain in King Philip's War 1675. Buried Memento Mori Cemetery.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington. FindAGrave confirms.

burialCemetery.notes: Historic burying ground in Farmington Connecticut, founded 1661 — one of the oldest cemeteries in the state. Resting place of John Mix (X00263, 1755-1834, Revolutionary War lieutenant and 32-year Farmington town clerk) and his wife Martha Cowles Mix (H00437, 1753-1826), with most of their eight children — Capt. Ebenezer Hooker Mix (the East India sailor character of Farmington), Betsey, Catherine Williams, John William, and Martha Hooker — alongside Ann Goodwin (the first, lost at age 3) and the boy William (lost at age 4). Ann Goodwin No. 2 also rests here.

crossConnections.display_label: Her second cousin Martha married her husband's older brother Ezekiel.
